Background

Decisions,
not commentary

Most petrochemical, refining and energy strategy gets written by people who've only ever seen one side of the chain — the trading desk, the plant, or the boardroom. I've worked all three: hedging US ethane cargoes into Reliance's import terminal, building the FP&A models that justified the capital, and now running full P&L for a national distribution business. That's the lens this research is written through — decisions with real capital and real counterparties attached, not market commentary.

I default to MECE breakdowns and SCQA framing to turn ambiguous industry questions into a structured set of options before committing to a recommendation — the same disciplined approach this entire research portfolio is built around.

My work sits at the convergence of commercial strategy, commodity economics, and energy transition, bringing engineering-level process understanding together with the financial and geopolitical lens of an industry practitioner.

The research on this site is written for strategic decision-makers across the US and Gulf energy ecosystem — addressing the capital allocation questions shaping the sector over the next decade.

Supply Dynamics Live

The GCC Polyolefins Paradox: Re-Anchoring the Feedstock Advantage in an Inverted Market

“The Gulf’s decades-old petrochemical advantage—cheap ethane converted into commodity polyolefins for Asian demand—is confronting a structural reversal as China transforms from the world’s largest importer into an aggressive exporter. As spot margins compress and trade barriers rise, this analysis explores why GCC producers must either pivot toward high-barrier circular value chains or deploy capital globally to secure downstream competitiveness.”

ICIS · S&P Global · McKinsey Chemicals →

01 / 10
The 2025 US Ethane Export Disruption

Supply chain signals and what comes next in 2026

In May 2025, US ethane exports to China — the world's largest bilateral feedstock trade — came to a sudden halt. Forty days later, they resumed. But the supply chain that paused didn't simply reset. Here is what the disruption revealed about global ethane trade architecture — and where it goes from here.
2.8M
b/d US ethane production, 2024 record
47%
of US exports absorbed by China in 2024
40
days the export halt lasted
$166M
estimated EBITDA at risk for ET + EPD
*ET = Energy Transfer · EPD = Enterprise Products Partners
Sources: EIA, East Daley Analytics, Baird Maritime
Aditya Pathak · Energy Strategy
02 / 10
What US ethane is and why it matters

Production surge, then a structural reversal

US ethane production rose from 1.8M b/d in 2019 to 2.8M b/d in 2024, projected to fall to 2.7M in 2025 and 2.4M in 2026.
+55%Production growth 2019–2024
~2.9M b/d2025 average (marginal gain)
−12% YoY2026 projected decline

The 2026 decline has two interlocked drivers. First, export licensing curbs reduced demand signals for ethane recovery. Second, ethane rejection: when export routes are constrained, processors leave ethane blended into the natural gas stream rather than separating it — a practice known as rejection into the pipeline grid. US crackers are already operating near maximum technical capacity and cannot absorb the displaced 200,000 b/d of physical supply.

Sources: EIA Short-Term Energy Outlook Oct 2025 · EIA June 2025 STEO
03 / 10
Vessel constraints and trade architecture

The fleet that moves ethane — and its structural limits

29
VLECs in service globally
25%
of fleet built in China
$165M
avg VLEC build cost
3 yrs
to build a VLEC

VLECs operate almost exclusively on dedicated long-term contracts tied to specific cracker supply chains. There is no meaningful spot market — a stranded cargo cannot be redirected to another buyer without an alternative receiving terminal and a compatible vessel. Distressed cargoes were sold at significant discounts. When geopolitical risk hits, the entire chain freezes simultaneously.

Compounding this: charter day rates spiked to $100,000–$400,000/day on the back of war risk premiums, Strait of Hormuz disruption concerns, and VLSFO bunker surcharges. Vessels were diverted via two longer routing options:

Panama Canal slots remain constrained by drought-driven water levels and Neopanamax congestion; the Cape of Good Hope route adds roughly 14–20 days versus the pre-disruption Suez baseline to India. Estimates use typical VLEC cruising speeds (~14–15 knots) and will vary with congestion and weather routing.

Eastern Pacific Shipping ordered 6 Ultra-Large Ethane Carriers (ULECs) in 2024, for 2027 delivery, also China-built. The US Trade Representative proposed ~$2M per-voyage fees on Chinese-built vessels — adding further friction to the dominant vessel class on the very trade route being disrupted.

Sources: EIA Oct 2025 · Reuters Feb 2025 · IINO Lines · Baird Maritime
04 / 10
Disruption sequence

The numbers behind the May–July 2025 export halt

MetricFigure
US ethane to China, 2024227,000 b/d
Share of total US exports47%
Vessels stranded, Jun 20257 VLECs
Volumes in limbo (2 vessels)~2M bbl
EPD denied emergency licenses3 cargoes / 2.2M bbl
ET EBITDA at risk$65M
EPD EBITDA at risk$101M
Projected export drop 202580,000 b/d
Projected export drop 2026177,000 b/d

US terminal expansion underway simultaneously:

Q2 2025 — ET Nederland, Texas
250,000 b/d capacity · Est. ~$2.5B investment
Jul 2025 — Enterprise Neches River, Texas
Phase 1: 120,000 b/d · ~$1.2B
Early 2026 — Neches River Phase 2
+180,000 b/d · ~$1.8B (est.)
Both terminals commissioned capacity during the same window exports to their primary market were suspended — exposing a timing paradox in US midstream investment planning.
Sources: EIA Jun 2025 · East Daley Analytics · Baird Maritime · BIS filing
05 / 10
The unplanned beneficiary

India steps in — two vessels redirected to Dahej

While seven VLECs sat idle off the US Gulf Coast, two vessels originally contracted for China were diverted to ethane crackers in Dahej, India. India's position was structurally ready — Reliance Industries commissioned a large-scale US ethane import terminal at Dahej in 2017, a decade ahead of most regional competitors.

Indian ethane playerStatusDetail
Reliance IndustriesOperationalDahej terminal operational since 2017 · received diverted cargoes Jun 2025
ONGC / OPaLPlanned800,000 t/yr ethane import plan from May 2028 · dual-feed Dahej cracker
SCG / Long Son (Vietnam)EvaluatingRe-configuring cracker for US ethane; watching India model
Formosa Petrochemical (Taiwan)StudyingLargest naphtha importer in SE Asia — evaluating ethane switch
India also gained diplomatically: growing US energy imports provided Indian trade negotiators material goodwill in parallel tariff discussions with Washington — a non-trivial benefit from what was, for China, an unwanted supply disruption.

Ethane crackers in China earned $300–500/tonne of ethylene vs naphtha — the economics of the feedstock switch are compelling. Sanjiang Chemical reported a one-fifth cost reduction and a flip from losses to profit after commissioning its mixed-feed cracker (H1 2024 financial report).

Sources: EIA Jun 2025 · ICIS · Business Standard · Reuters Feb 2025 (Energy Aspects, Cheryl Liu)
06 / 10
Restrictions lifted

40 days: what a temporary halt permanently revealed

May 23, 2025
BIS imposes export licensing requirements on all ethane shipments to China. Effective immediately. Justification: national security risk of military diversion.
Late May – Jun 2025
7 VLECs stranded off US Gulf Coast. EPD denied emergency authorization on 3 cargoes (~2.2M bbl). Satellite Petrochemical (China) — a pure-play ethane cracker — shuts down within weeks. Ethane spot prices collapse on domestic oversupply concerns.
June 2025
Two diverted VLECs arrive at Dahej, India. EIA projects 12% drop in US ethane production by 2026 if restrictions hold.
July 2, 2025
BIS rescinds licensing requirements. Reversal tied to US-China London trade talks: China removes barriers on rare earths; US lifts energy export controls. Exports resume.
"The US recovered its export volumes. But it revealed to every buyer in Asia that US ethane supply carries geopolitical counterparty risk — and that bilateral concentration at 47% is not a commercial risk, it is a structural one."
Sources: EIA · BIS filing · Bloomberg · CSIS · Oil & Gas 360 · East Daley Analytics
07 / 10
Structural vulnerabilities

Three problems that didn't resolve when restrictions lifted

1 · Vessel concentration risk
~25% of the global VLEC fleet is Chinese-built. US Trade Representative fees of ~$2M per voyage add cost friction to normal trade even after the dispute is resolved. The fleet that carries the commodity is itself embedded in the geopolitical tension.
2 · Single-supplier dependency for China
China depends almost entirely on the US for ethane imports. Unlike LNG, which has a global spot market and multiple suppliers, ethane trade is bilateral by design — the terminal, vessel, and cracker form a closed supply chain. There is no alternative supplier of scale. That dependency is now politically visible to Beijing in a way it wasn't before May 2025.
3 · Cracker lock-in: no feedstock flexibility
Ethane-only crackers cannot switch feedstock — they are purpose-built. Satellite Petrochemical shut down within weeks of the restriction. Sanjiang Chemical's mixed-feed cracker, by contrast, cut costs by one-fifth and turned profitable. The distinction between flexible and inflexible cracker design is now a balance sheet question, not a technical one.
Sources: EIA · Argus Media · Baird Maritime · Sanjiang Chemical H1 2024 Financial Report · ICIS
08 / 10
Strategic options

Three actors, three imperatives

For US midstream (ET, EPD)
Diversify the buyer base before the next trade cycle. India, Thailand, Vietnam, and Taiwan are all actively evaluating ethane imports. The Neches River expansion capacity should be contracted to non-China buyers as a structural hedge. A 47% single-buyer concentration is not a commercial negotiating advantage — it is a regulatory exposure waiting to be triggered again.
For Chinese producers (2026 outlook)
Accelerate transition to mixed-feed cracker configurations capable of switching between ethane and naphtha. China PP exports are now tracking toward 5.7M tonnes in 2025, up from 0.4M in 2020 — the downstream model is adapting. But upstream feedstock vulnerability remains. Mixed-feed flexibility is the near-term answer; reducing US dependency over a 5–7 year horizon is the strategic one.
For India (2026 outlook)
The window is open but not permanent. ONGC's 2028 ethane import timeline should be brought forward. The combination of lower ethane prices during disruption periods, US supply redirected from China, and diplomatic goodwill from being a reliable US energy partner creates a compressing window to lock in long-term supply agreements at favorable terms. Reliance's infrastructure is the proof of concept. Replication at scale is the play.
Sources: ICIS · EIA · Business Standard · Reuters · Argus Media
09 / 10
The consultant's take

What the 40-day disruption actually revealed

The US-China ethane episode is not really about ethane. It is a case study in what happens when a critical industrial feedstock becomes embedded in a bilateral trade relationship — and then surfaces as a diplomatic instrument.

The 40-day disruption caused more operational damage to Chinese crackers than years of tariff negotiation. A pure-play ethane cracker shut down within weeks. Two vessels found new homes in India almost immediately. That asymmetry will not be unlearned in Beijing.

Chinese producers will spend the next decade reducing ethane dependency through mixed-feed flexibility, even at higher short-run cost. Feedstock diversification will be priced into cracker investment decisions going forward. US exporters, for their part, now know that concentrating 47% of volumes in a single geopolitically contested counterparty is an investment risk, not a market strategy.

India, if it moves with deliberate speed, sits at the convergence of both trends: a US-aligned trade partner, a growing cracker base, and a non-contested supply lane. The question for Indian policymakers and producers is whether they treat the 2025 disruption as a windfall moment or as the starting point for a structured, long-term feedstock strategy.

Sources: EIA · ICIS · East Daley Analytics · Argus Media · BIS · CSIS
10 / 10
